schemaVersion: Ehel English Course Final Assessment v1.2
subject: English
title: Grade 8 English Final Course Quiz
description: A cumulative assessment of vocabulary, reading, language and communication across Units 1-9.

sections.title: Words and Meaning
sections.description: Use Grade 8 vocabulary accurately in familiar and new contexts.

sections.title: Reading and Language
sections.description: Show reading understanding and control of Grade 8 language patterns.

sections.title: Communication and Application
sections.description: Apply English to speaking, writing and purposeful communication.

questions.question: What does 'concise' mean?
questions.options: A statement that explains the meaning of a word. | A strong feeling of enthusiasm and eagerness. | The length of time that a person or thing lives or lasts. | Using few words to say something clearly.
questions.correctAnswer: Using few words to say something clearly.
questions.explanation: The teacher asked us to write a concise summary of the story.

questions.question: What does 'barricade' mean?
questions.options: A statement that explains the meaning of a word. | A structure used to block people or vehicles from passing. | The length of time that a person or thing lives or lasts. | To give or set aside a certain amount for a specific purpose.
questions.correctAnswer: A structure used to block people or vehicles from passing.
questions.explanation: The protesters built a barricade across the road using old crates.

questions.question: What does 'zeal' mean?
questions.options: A statement that explains the meaning of a word. | A strong feeling of enthusiasm and eagerness. | To give or set aside a certain amount for a specific purpose. | To make someone familiar with something through repeated experience.
questions.correctAnswer: A strong feeling of enthusiasm and eagerness.
questions.explanation: She studied with great zeal because she wanted the top grade.

questions.question: What does 'longevity' mean?
questions.options: A small argument about something unimportant. | The length of time that a person or thing lives or lasts. | To give or set aside a certain amount for a specific purpose. | To make someone familiar with something through repeated experience.
questions.correctAnswer: The length of time that a person or thing lives or lasts.
questions.explanation: Eating healthy food can improve your longevity.

questions.question: What does 'exult' mean?
questions.options: To come between two people or groups to help settle a problem or stop something bad. | To feel very happy and excited, especially because of a success or victory. | To pretend to have something or to be something that you are not. | To take control of something, especially a position of power, without the right to do so.
questions.correctAnswer: To feel very happy and excited, especially because of a success or victory.
questions.explanation: After winning the game, the team began to exult and celebrate their victory.

questions.question: What does 'loathe' mean?
questions.options: To block something so that it cannot move or be seen. | To come between two people or groups to help settle a problem or stop something bad. | To feel strong dislike or hatred for something or someone. | To pretend to have something or to be something that you are not.
questions.correctAnswer: To feel strong dislike or hatred for something or someone.
questions.explanation: I loathe doing homework on weekends when the weather is beautiful.

questions.question: What does 'usurp' mean?
questions.options: To block something so that it cannot move or be seen. | To come between two people or groups to help settle a problem or stop something bad. | To prevent something bad from happening. | To take control of something, especially a position of power, without the right to do so.
questions.correctAnswer: To take control of something, especially a position of power, without the right to do so.
questions.explanation: The student tried to usurp the class president's role by making all the decisions.

questions.question: What does 'feign' mean?
questions.options: To block something so that it cannot move or be seen. | To flood or overwhelm with a large amount of something. | To pretend to have something or to be something that you are not. | To prevent something bad from happening.
questions.correctAnswer: To pretend to have something or to be something that you are not.
questions.explanation: She tried to feign happiness, but everyone could see she was sad.

questions.question: What does 'stifle' mean?
questions.options: To express thoughts and ideas clearly and effectively. | To make something happen faster; to hurry. | To remember and honour someone or something, often with a ceremony. | To stop something from happening or developing; to hold back.
questions.correctAnswer: To stop something from happening or developing; to hold back.
questions.explanation: The teacher tried to stifle the noise in the classroom by asking everyone to be quiet.

questions.question: What does 'wane' mean?
questions.options: To become smaller, less strong, or less important over time. | To express thoughts and ideas clearly and effectively. | To make something happen faster; to hurry. | Verb: to publicly support or recommend. Noun: a person who supports a cause.
questions.correctAnswer: To become smaller, less strong, or less important over time.
questions.explanation: As the evening went on, the excitement at the party began to wane.

questions.question: What does 'commemorate' mean?
questions.options: To attach or stick something to another thing. | To make something happen faster; to hurry. | To remember and honour someone or something, often with a ceremony. | Verb: to publicly support or recommend. Noun: a person who supports a cause.
questions.correctAnswer: To remember and honour someone or something, often with a ceremony.
questions.explanation: We commemorate our school's anniversary every year with a big celebration.

questions.question: What does 'articulate' mean?
questions.options: To attach or stick something to another thing. | To express thoughts and ideas clearly and effectively. | To make something easier or help it happen. | Verb: to publicly support or recommend. Noun: a person who supports a cause.
questions.correctAnswer: To express thoughts and ideas clearly and effectively.
questions.explanation: She was able to articulate her opinion during the class discussion.

questions.question: What does 'aptitude' mean?
questions.options: A natural ability to do something well. | A situation or event that produces a large amount of something good. | Having the ability to achieve things in a clever and often deceptive way. | The force or speed of something moving, especially when it is getting faster.
questions.correctAnswer: A natural ability to do something well.
questions.explanation: She has a great aptitude for learning new languages quickly.

questions.question: What does 'adversary' mean?
questions.options: A person or group that opposes or fights against another. | A situation or event that produces a large amount of something good. | Something that is given up or allowed in order to reach an agreement. | The force or speed of something moving, especially when it is getting faster.
questions.correctAnswer: A person or group that opposes or fights against another.
questions.explanation: In the competition, each team tried to outsmart their adversary to win.

questions.question: What does 'cunning' mean?
questions.options: A situation or event that produces a large amount of something good. | An agreement or harmony between people or groups. | Having the ability to achieve things in a clever and often deceptive way. | Something that is given up or allowed in order to reach an agreement.
questions.correctAnswer: Having the ability to achieve things in a clever and often deceptive way.
questions.explanation: The cunning fox tricked the farmer and stole some chickens.

questions.question: What does 'momentum' mean?
questions.options: An agreement or harmony between people or groups. | Showing a rude and disrespectful attitude. | Something that is given up or allowed in order to reach an agreement. | The force or speed of something moving, especially when it is getting faster.
questions.correctAnswer: The force or speed of something moving, especially when it is getting faster.
questions.explanation: The car gained momentum as it rolled down the hill.

questions.question: What does 'falter' mean?
questions.options: a word that means the opposite of another word | lacking experience or sophistication; easily fooled or deceived | making someone believe something that is not true; misleading | to hesitate or lose strength when doing something
questions.correctAnswer: to hesitate or lose strength when doing something
questions.explanation: She began to falter in her speech when she saw the large crowd.

questions.question: What does 'pristine' mean?
questions.options: a plan or method used to achieve a goal | a word that means the opposite of another word | clean, fresh, and in its original condition; not spoiled or damaged | lacking experience or sophistication; easily fooled or deceived
questions.correctAnswer: clean, fresh, and in its original condition; not spoiled or damaged
questions.explanation: The lake was so pristine that we could see all the fish swimming in it.

questions.question: What does 'deceptive' mean?
questions.options: a plan or method used to achieve a goal | a word that means the opposite of another word | continuing to do something even when it is difficult or when others give up | making someone believe something that is not true; misleading
questions.correctAnswer: making someone believe something that is not true; misleading
questions.explanation: The magician's tricks were so deceptive that the audience could not figure them out.

questions.question: What does 'naive' mean?
questions.options: a change from one state or condition to another | a plan or method used to achieve a goal | continuing to do something even when it is difficult or when others give up | lacking experience or sophistication; easily fooled or deceived
questions.correctAnswer: lacking experience or sophistication; easily fooled or deceived
questions.explanation: She was so naive that she believed every story she heard.

questions.question: What does 'atrocity' mean?
questions.options: A very cruel or terrible act | The power or right to give orders and make decisions | To make someone decide not to do something | To stop doing something
questions.correctAnswer: A very cruel or terrible act
questions.explanation: The war led to many atrocities that shocked the world.

questions.question: What does 'destination' mean?
questions.options: A place where someone is going or something is being sent | A situation with no government or authority, leading to confusion | The power or right to give orders and make decisions | To make someone decide not to do something
questions.correctAnswer: A place where someone is going or something is being sent
questions.explanation: Our destination for the field trip is the national park.

questions.question: What does 'desist' mean?
questions.options: A situation with no government or authority, leading to confusion | Done secretly because you do not want to be noticed | The power or right to give orders and make decisions | To stop doing something
questions.correctAnswer: To stop doing something
questions.explanation: The teacher asked the students to desist from talking during the lesson.

questions.question: What does 'deter' mean?
questions.options: A situation with no government or authority, leading to confusion | Done secretly because you do not want to be noticed | To make someone angry or upset by your actions or words | To make someone decide not to do something
questions.correctAnswer: To make someone decide not to do something
questions.explanation: The warning signs deter hikers from taking the dangerous path.

questions.question: What does '1. benign' mean?
questions.options: A person's usual mood or way of thinking. | A problem or fault in something that makes it not work properly. | Done according to a plan or system; organised and methodical. | Kind and gentle; not harmful or dangerous.
questions.correctAnswer: Kind and gentle; not harmful or dangerous.
questions.explanation: The teacher's benign smile made the students feel comfortable in class.

questions.question: What does '2. reprieve' mean?
questions.options: A hidden store of things, often used to keep items safe. | A person's usual mood or way of thinking. | A temporary break from something difficult or unpleasant. | Done according to a plan or system; organised and methodical.
questions.correctAnswer: A temporary break from something difficult or unpleasant.
questions.explanation: After studying hard for exams, I took a reprieve by playing video games for an hour.

questions.question: What does '3. defect' mean?
questions.options: A hidden store of things, often used to keep items safe. | A problem or fault in something that makes it not work properly. | Done according to a plan or system; organised and methodical. | Requiring a lot of effort and energy.
questions.correctAnswer: A problem or fault in something that makes it not work properly.
questions.explanation: The toy had a defect, so it did not work when we tried to use it.

questions.question: What does '4. disposition' mean?
questions.options: A hidden store of things, often used to keep items safe. | A person's usual mood or way of thinking. | Requiring a lot of effort and energy. | The final part of something where you summarise or decide what it means.
questions.correctAnswer: A person's usual mood or way of thinking.
questions.explanation: Her cheerful disposition makes everyone around her feel happy.

questions.question: What does 'crusade' mean?
questions.options: A strong effort to achieve something important, often related to a cause or belief. | Something that has no limits or end; it goes on forever. | To attack someone or something violently or with strong words. | To make someone want to do something or work harder.
questions.correctAnswer: A strong effort to achieve something important, often related to a cause or belief.
questions.explanation: The students started a crusade to raise money for their school library.

questions.question: What does 'clarify' mean?
questions.options: Something that has no limits or end; it goes on forever. | The ability to keep going and not get tired easily. | To make someone want to do something or work harder. | To make something clear or easier to understand.
questions.correctAnswer: To make something clear or easier to understand.
questions.explanation: Can you clarify what you mean by that?
